subject: ap-csp
theme: The internet is a fault-tolerant, packet-switched network of networks — built on protocols, redundancy, and distributed routing — that enables scalable global communication.

core_concepts.title: Internet Protocols and the Web
core_concepts.detail: The internet is built on protocols — agreed-upon rules like IP (routing), TCP (reliable delivery), HTTP (web pages), and DNS (translating domain names to IP addresses). The World Wide Web is a service running on the internet, not the internet itself.

core_concepts.title: Packets and Packet Switching
core_concepts.detail: Data is broken into packets, each containing source/destination IP addresses and a portion of the message. Packets are switched independently — each router decides the best next hop based on current network conditions, so packets from the same message can travel different routes.

core_concepts.title: TCP Reliability and DNS Resolution
core_concepts.detail: TCP ensures reliable delivery by acknowledging packets and retransmitting any that are lost. DNS resolves human-readable domain names (like google.com) into numeric IP addresses that routers use to direct traffic.

core_concepts.title: Redundancy and Fault Tolerance
core_concepts.detail: Redundancy — multiple paths between network nodes — makes the internet fault tolerant. If one router or link fails, packets are automatically rerouted through an alternative path. A network with only one path between nodes is a single point of failure.

core_concepts.title: Fault Tolerance and Scalability
core_concepts.detail: Fault tolerance means a system continues operating correctly even when some components fail. Scalability means a system can handle growing load without significant performance loss. Both are design goals for robust internet infrastructure.

core_concepts.title: Parallel vs. Distributed Computing
core_concepts.detail: Parallel computing runs multiple tasks simultaneously on multiple processors within one machine, reducing total execution time for divisible tasks. Distributed computing divides a task across many separate networked computers, enabling problems too large for any single machine.

core_concepts.title: Speedup Limits in Parallel Computing
core_concepts.detail: Parallel speedup is limited by the sequential (non-parallelizable) portions of a task. Even with unlimited processors, the parts of a program that must run in order cannot be parallelized — so total speedup is bounded by how much of the task is truly independent. Tasks that are fully independent can achieve near-linear speedup with more processors.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests fault tolerance (redundant paths vs. single points of failure) and parallel computing speedup — remember that adding processors does not produce proportional speedup because sequential portions limit maximum parallelism.
